answer:
**Hospitality (*atithyam*)** in the Smṛti corpus refers to the ritualized reception and feeding of an *atithi*—typically an unexpected guest, especially a wandering Brāhmaṇa from outside one's village, who arrives without prior notice (often during the *Vaiśvadeva* rite) and stays only one night (*anitya*, "without fixed time").[^1] [^2] [^3] It is not mere politeness but a sacred duty akin to worshiping a deity or Viṣṇu himself, demanding immediate honor through *arghya* water, a seat, foot-washing, pleasant conversation, food (even if the host must fast), and an escort upon departure.[^4] [^5] Neglect brings severe consequences: the householder's ancestors refuse offerings for 15 years, fire rituals become futile, and the negligent fall to hell (reborn as a crow).[^6] Even sinners or outcastes qualify as *atithi* if arriving at the right time.[^7] Non-Brāhmaṇas (Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, Śūdra) may receive lesser hospitality after Brāhmaṇas eat.[^8]

Among householder (*gṛhastha*) duties, *atithyam* ranks as one of the **five or six *mahāyajñas* (great sacrifices)**—daily, indispensable rites preventing one from becoming an *apaca* (neglectful householder). These are: (1) *bhūta-yajña* (to beings, via *bali* offerings), (2) *pitṛ-yajña* (to ancestors), (3) *deva-yajña* (to gods, via *homa*), (4) *brahma-yajña* (Veda study), and (5) *atithi- or manuṣya-yajña* (guests); some texts add *Vaiśvadeva* as sixth.[^9] [^10] [^11] It equals or surpasses other sacrifices: "By the study of the Veda, by the Agnihotra... a householder does not attain those worlds which he does by the worship of an *atithi*."[^12] The householder eats last, after gods, ancestors, guests, dependents, and servants.[^13] This holds across Pārāśara, Yājñavalkya, Viṣṇu, and Manu Smṛtis for Kali *yuga* householders.[^14]

The corpus (37 works) lacks exhaustive coverage of all Smṛtis (e.g., no full Gautama or Baudhāyana), but these texts consistently elevate it as foundational *gṛhastha-dharma*.

[^1]: Pārāśara Smṛti 1.41–42, [paragraph](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-20eb98).

[^2]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti 1.111, [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-6cfa89).

[^3]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 67.34–35, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/#p-653828).

[^4]: Pārāśara Smṛti 1.43–44, [paragraph](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-189326).

[^5]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 67.28–30, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/#p-a3a277).

[^6]: Pārāśara Smṛti 1.45–46, 56–57, [passage](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-d461ee).

[^7]: Pārāśara Smṛti 1.58, [paragraph](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-47bae3).

[^8]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 67.36–37, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/#p-653828).

[^9]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti 1.102, [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-76e5e9).

[^10]: Pārāśara Smṛti 1.39, [paragraph](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-40daa2).

[^11]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 67 (whole chapter on daily rites), [chapter](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/).

[^12]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 67.44, [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/#p-653828).

[^13]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 67.41–43, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/#p-653828).

[^14]: Manusmṛti 3.98–102, [passage](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-de0b29) (searches confirm alignment).

args.query: "great sacrifices" OR mahayajna OR "five sacrifices" OR "pancha mahayajna" OR "pancha maha" hospitality OR guest OR atithi
